**Ramla** ([Hebrew](/wiki/Hebrew_language "Hebrew language"): רַמְלָה, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Hebrew "Romanization of Hebrew"): *Ramlā*), also known as **Ramle** ([Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language "Arabic language"): الرملة, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Arabic "Romanization of Arabic"): *ar-Ramla*, [local pronunciation](/wiki/Palestinian_Arabic "Palestinian Arabic"): [[ɪrˈrɑmle]](/wiki/Help:IPA "Help:IPA")),[[a]](#cite_note-2) is a city in the [Central District](/wiki/Central_District_(Israel) "Central District (Israel)") of [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel"). Ramle is one of Israel's [mixed cities](/wiki/Mixed_cities "Mixed cities"), with significant numbers of both Jews and [Arabs](/wiki/Israeli_Arabs "Israeli Arabs").[[2]](#cite_note-:0-3)

The city was founded in the early 8th century CE by the [Umayyad](/wiki/Umayyad_Caliphate "Umayyad Caliphate") [caliph](/wiki/Caliph "Caliph") [Sulayman ibn Abd al-Malik](/wiki/Sulayman_ibn_Abd_al-Malik "Sulayman ibn Abd al-Malik") as the capital of [Jund Filastin](/wiki/Jund_Filastin "Jund Filastin"), the district he governed in [Bilad al-Sham](/wiki/Bilad_al-Sham "Bilad al-Sham") before becoming [caliph](/wiki/Caliphate "Caliphate") in 715. The city's strategic and economic value derived from its location at the intersection of the *[Via Maris](/wiki/Via_Maris "Via Maris")*, connecting [Cairo](/wiki/Cairo "Cairo") with [Damascus](/wiki/Damascus "Damascus"), and the road connecting the [Mediterranean](/wiki/Mediterranean_Sea "Mediterranean Sea") port of [Jaffa](/wiki/Jaffa "Jaffa") with [Jerusalem](/wiki/Jerusalem "Jerusalem").[[3]](#cite_note-4) It rapidly overshadowed the adjacent city of [Lydda](/wiki/Lod "Lod"), whose inhabitants were relocated to the new city. Not long after its establishment, Ramla developed as the commercial centre of [Palestine](/wiki/Palestine_(region) "Palestine (region)"), serving as a hub for pottery, dyeing, weaving, and olive oil, and as the home of numerous [Muslim scholars](/wiki/Ulama "Ulama"). Its prosperity was lauded by geographers in the 10th–11th centuries, when the city was ruled by the [Fatimids](/wiki/Fatimid_Caliphate "Fatimid Caliphate") and [Seljuks](/wiki/Seljuk_Empire "Seljuk Empire").

It lost its role as a provincial capital shortly before the arrival of the [first crusaders](/wiki/First_crusade "First crusade") (c. 1099), after which it became the scene of [various battles](/wiki/Battle_of_Ramla_(disambiguation) "Battle of Ramla (disambiguation)") between the crusaders and Fatimids in the first years of the 12th century. Later that century, it became the centre of a [lordship](/wiki/Lordship_of_Ramla "Lordship of Ramla") in the [Kingdom of Jerusalem](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Jerusalem "Kingdom of Jerusalem"), a [crusader state](/wiki/Crusader_state "Crusader state") established by [Godfrey of Bouillon](/wiki/Godfrey_of_Bouillon "Godfrey of Bouillon"). The city declined in the [Ottoman](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") period, and 19th-century visitors described it as a town of only a few thousand inhabitants in a state of decay.

Ramla had an [Arab](/wiki/Arabs "Arabs")-majority population before most [were expelled](/wiki/1948_Palestinian_expulsion_from_Lydda_and_Ramle "1948 Palestinian expulsion from Lydda and Ramle") during the [1948 Arab–Israeli War](/wiki/1948_Arab%E2%80%93Israeli_War "1948 Arab–Israeli War").[[4]](#cite_note-5) The town was subsequently repopulated by [Jewish](/wiki/Jews "Jews") immigrants. Today, Ramla is one of Israel's [mixed cities](/wiki/Mixed_cities "Mixed cities"), with a population 76% Jewish and 24% Arab.[[1]](#cite_note-Israelpopulations-1)[[2]](#cite_note-:0-3)

## History

### Umayyad period

[![A colorized skyline of a town with hills on the horizon, white buildings in the background, and a cemetery, olive trees, and dirt paths in the foreground](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/Flickr_-_%E2%80%A6trialsanderrors_-_View_from_Tower_of_the_Forty_Martyrs%2C_Ramleh%2C_Holy_Land%2C_ca._1895.jpg/330px-Flickr_-_%E2%80%A6trialsanderrors_-_View_from_Tower_of_the_Forty_Martyrs%2C_Ramleh%2C_Holy_Land%2C_ca._1895.jpg)](/wiki/File:Flickr_-_%E2%80%A6trialsanderrors_-_View_from_Tower_of_the_Forty_Martyrs,_Ramleh,_Holy_Land,_ca._1895.jpg)

Ramla (pictured in 1895) was founded by Sulayman at the start of the 8th century and became the capital of his district.

The [Umayyad](/wiki/Umayyad_Caliphate "Umayyad Caliphate") prince and governor of [Jund Filasṭīn](/wiki/Jund_Filastin "Jund Filastin"), [Sulayman ibn Abd al-Malik](/wiki/Sulayman_ibn_Abd_al-Malik "Sulayman ibn Abd al-Malik"), founded Ramla as the seat of his administration,[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEisener1997821-6)[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBacharach199635-7)[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELuz199752-8) replacing [Lydda](/wiki/Lod "Lod"), the Muslims' original provincial capital.[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEisener1997821-6)[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBacharach199635-7) Sulayman had been appointed governor by his father Caliph [Abd al-Malik](/wiki/Abd_al-Malik_ibn_Marwan "Abd al-Malik ibn Marwan") before the end of his reign in 705 and continued in office through the reign of his brother Caliph [al-Walid I](/wiki/Al-Walid_I "Al-Walid I") (r. 705–715), whom he succeeded.[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEisener1997821-6) He died as caliph in 717. Ramla remained the capital of Jund Filasṭīn through the [Fatimid](/wiki/Fatimid_Caliphate "Fatimid Caliphate") period (10th–11th centuries).[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETaxel2013161-9) Its role as the principal city and district capital came to an end shortly before the arrival of the [First Crusaders](/wiki/First_Crusade "First Crusade") in 1099.[[9]](#cite_note-10) It received its name, the singular form of *raml* (sand), from the sandy area in which it sat.[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHonigmann19871116-11)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/Ramla_6799-3a.jpg/250px-Ramla_6799-3a.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ramla_6799-3a.jpg)

Ramla's Pool of Arches, a tourist destination

Sulayman's motives for founding Ramla were personal ambition and practical considerations. The location of Ramla near Lydda, a long-established and prosperous city, was logistically and economically advantageous.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELuz199752–53-12) The area's economic importance was based on its strategic location at the intersection of the two major roads linking [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt") with [Syria](/wiki/Syria_(region) "Syria (region)") (the so-called "[Via Maris](/wiki/Via_Maris "Via Maris")") and linking Jerusalem with the Mediterranean coast.[[12]](#cite_note-13) Sulayman established his city in Lydda's vicinity, avoiding Lydda proper. This was likely due to a lack of available space for wide-scale development and agreements dating to the [Muslim conquest](/wiki/Muslim_conquest_of_the_Levant "Muslim conquest of the Levant") in the 630s that, at least formally, precluded him from confiscating desirable property within Lydda.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELuz199752–53-12)

In a tradition recorded by the historian [Ibn Fadlallah al-Umari](/wiki/Ibn_Fadlallah_al-Umari "Ibn Fadlallah al-Umari") (died 1347), a determined local Christian cleric refused Sulayman's requests for plots in the middle of Lydda. Infuriated, he attempted to have the cleric executed, but his local adviser [Raja ibn Haywa](/wiki/Raja_ibn_Haywa "Raja ibn Haywa") dissuaded him and instead proposed building a new city at a superior, adjacent site.[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELuz199748-14) In choosing the site, Sulayman utilized the strategic advantages of Lydda's vicinity while avoiding the physical constraints of an already-established urban center.[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELuz199753-15) Historian [Moshe Sharon](/wiki/Moshe_Sharon "Moshe Sharon") holds that Lydda was "too Christian in ethos for the taste of the Umayyad rulers", particularly following the [Arabization and Islamization reforms](/wiki/Abd_al-Malik_ibn_Marwan#Legacy "Abd al-Malik ibn Marwan") instituted by Abd al-Malik.[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESharon1986799-16)

According to [al-Jahshiyari](/wiki/Al-Jahshiyari "Al-Jahshiyari") (died 942), Sulayman sought a lasting reputation as a great builder following the example of his father and al-Walid, the respective founders of the [Dome of the Rock](/wiki/Dome_of_the_Rock "Dome of the Rock") in [Jerusalem](/wiki/Jerusalem "Jerusalem") and the [Great Mosque of Damascus](/wiki/Umayyad_Mosque "Umayyad Mosque"). The construction of Ramla was Sulayman's "way to immortality" and "his personal stamp on the landscape of Palestine", according to Luz.[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELuz199747,_53–54-17)

[![White stone ruins of a building, including arches and columns, in a grassy area with modern, high-rise buildings in the background](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/PikiWiki_Israel_41123_White_Mosque_Ramla.JPG/330px-PikiWiki_Israel_41123_White_Mosque_Ramla.JPG)](/wiki/File:PikiWiki_Israel_41123_White_Mosque_Ramla.JPG)

Remains of the [White Mosque](/wiki/White_Mosque_(Ramla) "White Mosque (Ramla)") in Ramla (pictured in 2014) built by Sulayman and his cousin and successor [Umar II](/wiki/Umar_II "Umar II")

The first structure Sulayman erected in Ramla was his palatial residence,[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELuz199753-15) which dually served as the seat of Jund Filasṭīn's administration (*[diwan](/wiki/Divan "Divan")*). The next structure was the Dar al-Sabbaghin (House of the Dyers). At the center of the new city was a [congregational mosque](/wiki/Congregational_mosque "Congregational mosque"), later known as the [White Mosque](/wiki/White_Mosque_(Ramla) "White Mosque (Ramla)").[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELuz199737–38,_41–43-18) It was not completed until the reign of Sulayman's successor Caliph [Umar II](/wiki/Umar_II "Umar II") (r. 717–720).[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBacharach199627,_35–36-19) The Sulayman's construction works were financially managed by a Christian from Lydda, Bitrik ibn al-Naka.[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHonigmann19871116-11) The remains of the White Mosque, dominated by a minaret added at a later date, are visible in the present day. In the courtyard are underground water cisterns from the Umayyad period.[[19]](#cite_note-20) From early on, Ramla developed economically as a market town for the surrounding area's agricultural products, and as a center for dyeing, weaving and pottery. It was also home to many [Muslim religious scholars](/wiki/Ulema "Ulema").[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELuz199743–45-21)

Sulayman built an aqueduct in the city called al-Barada, which transported water to Ramla from [Tel Gezer](/wiki/Tel_Gezer "Tel Gezer"), about 10 kilometers (6 mi) to the southeast.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELuz199738–39-22) Ramla superseded Lydda as the commercial center of Palestine. Many of Lydda's Christian, [Samaritan](/wiki/Samaritan "Samaritan") and Jewish inhabitants were moved to the new city.[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELuz199742–43-23) Although the traditional accounts are in agreement that Lydda almost immediately fell into obscurity following the founding of Ramla, narratives vary about the extent of Sulayman's efforts to transfer Lydda's inhabitants to Ramla, some holding that he only demolished a church in Lydda and others that he demolished the city altogether.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBacharach199635-7) [Al-Ya'qubi](/wiki/Al-Ya%27qubi "Al-Ya'qubi") (died 839) noted Sulayman razed the houses of Lydda's inhabitants to force their relocation to Ramla and punished those who resisted.[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGordonRobinsonRowsonFishbein20181005-24)[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESharon1986800-25) In the words of al-Jahshiyari, Sulayman "founded the town of al-Ramla and its mosque and thus caused the ruin of Lod [Lydda]".[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELuz199747-26)

### Abbasid period

The [Abbasids](/wiki/Abbasid_Caliphate "Abbasid Caliphate") toppled the Umayyads in 750, confiscating the White Mosque and all other Umayyad properties in Ramla. The Abbasids annually reviewed the high costs of maintaining the Barada canal, though starting under the reign of Caliph [al-Mu'tasim](/wiki/Al-Mu%27tasim "Al-Mu'tasim") it became a regular part of the state's expenditures. In the late 9th century the Muslim inhabitants were composed mainly of Arabs and Persians, while the clients of the Muslims were Samaritans.[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHonigmann19871116-11)

The golden age of Ramla under the Umayyads and Abbasids, when the city overtook Jerusalem as a trade center, later gave way to a period of political instability and war beginning in the late 10th century. The Egypt-based Fatimids conquered Ramla in 969 and ten years later the city was destroyed by the [Jarrahids](/wiki/Jarrahids "Jarrahids"), a branch of the [Tayy](/wiki/Banu_Tayy "Banu Tayy") tribe.[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPetersen2005345–346-27)

Nonetheless, the 10th-century Jerusalemite geographer [al-Muqaddasi](/wiki/Al-Muqaddasi "Al-Muqaddasi") described Ramla as "a fine city, and well built; its water is good and plentiful; it fruits are abundant". He noted that it "combines manifold advantages, situated as it is in the midst of beautiful villages and lordly towns, near to holy places and pleasant hamlets", as well as bountiful fields, walled towns and hospices. The geographer further noted the city's significant commerce and "excellent markets", lauding the quality of its fruits and bread as the best of their kind.[[27]](#cite_note-28)[[28]](#cite_note-29) During this period, Ramla was one of the major centers for the production and export of oil extracted from unripe olives, known as *anfa kinon* ([Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language"): ὀμφάκιον, ὀμφάχινον; [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin"): omphacium; [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language "Arabic language"): زيت الأنفاق), and used in cuisine and medicine.[[29]](#cite_note-30)[[30]](#cite_note-31)

Conversely, the city's disadvantages included the severe muddiness of the place during the rainy winter season and its hard, sandy grounds due to its distance from natural water sources. The limited drinking water gathered in the city's cisterns were generally inaccessible to the poorer inhabitants.[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHonigmann19871116-11)

By 1011–1012, the Jarrahids controlled all of Palestine, except for the coastal towns, and captured Ramla from its Fatimid garrison, making it their capital. The city and the surrounding places were plundered by the Bedouin, impoverishing much of the population. The Jarrahids brought the [Alid](/wiki/Alids "Alids") [emir of Mecca](/wiki/Emir_of_Mecca "Emir of Mecca"), [al-Hasan ibn Ja'far](/wiki/Abu%27l-Futuh_al-Hasan_ibn_Ja%27far "Abu'l-Futuh al-Hasan ibn Ja'far"), to act as caliph in defiance of the Fatimids.[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGil1997381–382-32) The development was short-lived, as the Jarrahids abandoned al-Hasan after Fatimid bribes, and the caliphal claimant left the city for Mecca.[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKennedy2016286-33) A Fatimid army led by [Ali ibn Ja'far ibn Fallah](/wiki/Ali_ibn_Ja%27far_ibn_Fallah "Ali ibn Ja'far ibn Fallah") wrested control of Ramla from the Jarrahids, who continued to dominate the surrounding countryside.[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGil1997384-34) The following decade was marked by peace, but, in 1024, the Jarrahids renewed their rebellion. The Fatimid general [Anushtakin al-Dizbari](/wiki/Anushtakin_al-Dizbari "Anushtakin al-Dizbari") secured Ramla for a few months, but the Jarrahids overran the city that year, killing and harassing several inhabitants and seizing much of the population's wealth. They appointed their own governor, Nasr Allah ibn Nizal. In the following year, al-Dizbari drove the Jarrahids out of Ramla, but was recalled to Egypt in 1026. In 1029, he returned and routed the Jarrahids and their Bedouin allies.[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGil1997388–391,_396–397-35)

Persian geographer [Nasir-i-Khusrau](/wiki/Nasir-i-Khusrau "Nasir-i-Khusrau") visited the city in 1047, remarking:

> Ramla is a great city, with strong walls built of stone, mortared, of great height and thickness, with iron gates opening therein. From the town to the sea-coast is a distance of three leagues. The inhabitants get their water from the rainfall, and in each house is a tank for storing the same, in order that there may always be a supply. In the middle of the Friday Mosque [White Mosque], also, is a large tank: and from it, when it is filled with water, anyone who wishes may take. The area of the mosque measures two hundred paces (*Gam*) by three hundred. Over one of its porches (*suffah*) is an inscription stating that on the 15th of [Muharram](/wiki/Muharram "Muharram"), of the year 425 (=10th of December, 1033 CE), there came an [earthquake](/wiki/1033_Jordan_Rift_Valley_earthquake "1033 Jordan Rift Valley earthquake")[[35]](#cite_note-36) of great violence, which threw down a large number of buildings, but that no single person sustained an injury. In the city of Ramla there is marble in plenty, and most of the buildings and private houses are of this material; and, further, the surface thereof they do most beautifully sculpture and ornament. They cut the marble here with a toothless saw, which is worked with 'Mekka sand'. They saw the marble in length, as is the case with wood, to form the columns; not in across; they also cut it into slabs. The marbles that I saw here were of all colours, some variegated, some green, red, black and white. There is, too, at Ramla, a particular kind of fig, and this they export to all the countries round. This city Ramla, throughout Syria and the West, is known under the name of Filastin.[[36]](#cite_note-37)[[37]](#cite_note-38)

### Crusader period

Main article: [Lordship of Ramla](/wiki/Lordship_of_Ramla "Lordship of Ramla")

The armies of the First Crusade took the hastily evacuated town without a fight. In the early years of the Crusader [Kingdom of Jerusalem](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Jerusalem "Kingdom of Jerusalem") though, control over this strategic location led to three consecutive [battles](/wiki/Battle_of_Ramla_(disambiguation) "Battle of Ramla (disambiguation)") between the Crusaders and Egyptian armies from [Ascalon](/wiki/Ashkelon "Ashkelon"), a Fatimid-held town along the southern coast of Palestine. As Crusader rule stabilized, Ramla became the seat of a [seigneury](/wiki/Seigneury "Seigneury") in the Kingdom of Jerusalem, the [Lordship of Ramla](/wiki/Lordship_of_Ramla "Lordship of Ramla") within the [County of Jaffa and Ascalon](/wiki/County_of_Jaffa_and_Ascalon "County of Jaffa and Ascalon"). It was a city of some economic significance and an important way station for pilgrims travelling to Jerusalem. The Crusaders identified it with the biblical [Ramathaim](/wiki/Ramathaim-Zophim "Ramathaim-Zophim") and called it [Arimathea](/wiki/Arimathea "Arimathea").[[38]](#cite_note-39)[[39]](#cite_note-Pringle181-40)

Around 1163, the rabbi and traveller [Benjamin of Tudela](/wiki/Benjamin_of_Tudela "Benjamin of Tudela"), who also mistook it for a more ancient city, visited "[Rama](/wiki/Ramah_in_Benjamin "Ramah in Benjamin"), or Ramleh, where there are remains of the walls from the days of our ancestors, for thus it was found written upon the stones. About 300 Jews dwell there. It was formerly a very great city; at a distance of two miles (3 km) there is a large Jewish cemetery."[[40]](#cite_note-Adler-41)

### Late medieval period

In the 1480s, in the late [Mamluk](/wiki/Mamluk_Sultanate "Mamluk Sultanate") era, [Felix Fabri](/wiki/Felix_Fabri "Felix Fabri") visited Ramla and described (among other things) the [hammam](/wiki/Hammam "Hammam") there; "built in a wonderous and clever fashion".[[41]](#cite_note-42)

In the early days of the Ottoman period, in 1548, a census was taken recording 528 Muslim families and 82 Christian families living in Ramla.[[42]](#cite_note-43)[[43]](#cite_note-44)[[44]](#cite_note-Petersen95-45)

On [2 March 1799](/wiki/French_campaign_in_Egypt_and_Syria "French campaign in Egypt and Syria"), [Napoleon Bonaparte](/wiki/Napoleon_Bonaparte "Napoleon Bonaparte") occupied Ramla during his unsuccessful bid to conquer Palestine, using the Franciscan hospice as his headquarters.[[45]](#cite_note-46) The village appeared as 'Ramleh' on the map of [Pierre Jacotin](/wiki/Pierre_Jacotin "Pierre Jacotin") compiled during this campaign.[[46]](#cite_note-47)

In 1838, [Edward Robinson](/wiki/Edward_Robinson_(scholar) "Edward Robinson (scholar)") found Ramleh to be a town of about 3000 inhabitants, surrounded by olive-groves and vegetables. It had few streets, and the houses were made of stone and were well-built. There were several mosques in the town. Robinson noted that the city was about one-third Greek Orthodox Christians, and also had a substantial Armenian Christian population.[[47]](#cite_note-Robinson-48)

In 1863, [Victor Guérin](/wiki/Victor_Gu%C3%A9rin "Victor Guérin") noted that the Latin (Catholic) population was reduced to two priests and 50 parishioners.[[48]](#cite_note-49) In 1869, the population was given as 3,460; 3000 Muslims, 400 Greek Orthodox and 60 Catholics.[[49]](#cite_note-SWP252-50)

In 1872, Ramleh was visited by British explorer [Claude R. Conder](/wiki/Claude_Reignier_Conder "Claude Reignier Conder"), who wrote: "Ramleh, like many another town in this ruined land, is full of contrasts of past grandeur and of present squalor and decay. The walls of fine stone houses are enclosed in wretched hovels of mud. Here and there an ornate [Cufic](/wiki/Kufic "Kufic") or Arabic inscription is left, telling of Moslem conquerors and magnificent Caliphs; but the bazaars are deserted, and starved dogs and helpless [lepers](/wiki/Leprosy "Leprosy") meet the eye on every side."[[50]](#cite_note-51)

In 1882, the [Palestine Exploration Fund](/wiki/Palestine_Exploration_Fund "Palestine Exploration Fund")'s *Survey of Western Palestine* noted that there was a bazaar in the town, "but its prosperity has much decayed, and many of the houses are falling into ruins, including the [Serai](/wiki/Saray_(building) "Saray (building)")."[[51]](#cite_note-SWP253-52) Expansion began only at the end of the 19th century.[[52]](#cite_note-53)

In 1889, 31 Jewish worker families settled in the town, which had no Jewish population at the time.[[53]](#cite_note-54)

### British Mandate period

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/Air_views_of_Palestine._Air_route_over_Cana_of_Galilee%2C_Nazareth%2C_Plain_of_Sharon%2C_etc._Ramleh._Arimathea._Closer_view_LOC_matpc.15874.jpg/250px-Air_views_of_Palestine._Air_route_over_Cana_of_Galilee%2C_Nazareth%2C_Plain_of_Sharon%2C_etc._Ramleh._Arimathea._Closer_view_LOC_matpc.15874.jpg)](/wiki/File:Air_views_of_Palestine._Air_route_over_Cana_of_Galilee,_Nazareth,_Plain_of_Sharon,_etc._Ramleh._Arimathea._Closer_view_LOC_matpc.15874.jpg)

Ramla from the air in 1932

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/71/Er_Ramle_1941.jpg/250px-Er_Ramle_1941.jpg)](/wiki/File:Er_Ramle_1941.jpg)

Ramla 1941 1:20,000

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/66/El_Ramle_1945.jpg/250px-El_Ramle_1945.jpg)](/wiki/File:El_Ramle_1945.jpg)

Ramla 1945 1:250,000

In the [1922 census of Palestine](/wiki/1922_census_of_Palestine "1922 census of Palestine") conducted by the [British Mandate authorities](/wiki/Mandate_for_Palestine "Mandate for Palestine"), 'Ramleh' had a population of 7,312 (5,837 [Muslims](/wiki/Muslim "Muslim"), 1,440 Christians and 35 Jews).[[54]](#cite_note-Census1922a-55) The Christians were further noted by denomination: 1,226 [Orthodox](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church"), 2 [Syriac Orthodox](/wiki/Syriac_Orthodox_Church "Syriac Orthodox Church") (Jacobites), 150 [Roman Catholics](/wiki/Roman_Catholics "Roman Catholics"), 8 [Melkite Catholics](/wiki/Melkite_Catholic "Melkite Catholic"), 4 [Maronite](/wiki/Maronite_Catholic_Church "Maronite Catholic Church"), 15 [Armenian](/wiki/Armenian_Apostolic_Church "Armenian Apostolic Church"), 2 [Abyssinian Church](/wiki/Abyssinian_Church "Abyssinian Church") and 36 [Anglicans](/wiki/Anglicanism "Anglicanism").[[55]](#cite_note-Census1922b-56)

Less than a decade later, the population had increased nearly 25%; the [1931 census](/wiki/1931_census_of_Palestine "1931 census of Palestine") recorded 10,347 people (8,157 Muslims, 2,194 Christians, five Jews, and two [Druze](/wiki/Druze "Druze")), in a total of 2,339 houses.[[56]](#cite_note-Census1931-57)

Ramla was connected to wired electricity (supplied by the Zionist-owned [Palestine Electric Company](/wiki/Israel_Electric_Corporation#History "Israel Electric Corporation")) towards the end of the 1920s. Economist Basim Faris noted this fact as proof of Ramla's higher standard of living than neighbouring Lydda. In Ramla, he wrote, "economic demands triumph over nationalism" while Lydda, "which is ten minutes' walk from Ramleh, is still averse to such a convenience as electric current, and so is not as yet served; perhaps the low standard of living of the poor population prevents the use of the service at the present rates, which cannot compete with petroleum for lighting".[[57]](#cite_note-58)

[Sheikh Mustafa al-Khairi](/w/index.php?title=Sheikh_Mustafa_al-Khairi&action=edit&redlink=1 "Sheikh Mustafa al-Khairi (page does not exist)") was mayor of Ramla from 1920 to 1947.[[58]](#cite_note-59)

The 1938 village statistics list the population ("Ramle, Er") as 11,950.[[59]](#cite_note-60)

The 1945/46 survey gives 'Ramle' a population of 15,160 (11,900 Muslims and 3,260 Christians).[[60]](#cite_note-61)

### 1947–48 war

Main article: [1948 Palestinian expulsion from Lydda and Ramle](/wiki/1948_Palestinian_expulsion_from_Lydda_and_Ramle "1948 Palestinian expulsion from Lydda and Ramle")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/06/Ramla_i.jpg/250px-Ramla_i.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ramla_i.jpg)

Ramleh from air, 1948

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/RefugeesEscortedFromRamlaOperationDanny.jpg/250px-RefugeesEscortedFromRamlaOperationDanny.jpg)](/wiki/File:RefugeesEscortedFromRamlaOperationDanny.jpg)

Arab Palestinian residents of Ramleh being expelled by the Israeli army in July 1948

Ramla was part of the territory allotted to a proposed Arab state under the [1947 UN Partition Plan](/wiki/1947_UN_Partition_Plan "1947 UN Partition Plan").[[61]](#cite_note-62) However, Ramla's geographic location and its strategic position on the main supply route to Jerusalem made it a point of contention during the [1947–1948 civil war](/wiki/1947%E2%80%931948_civil_war_in_Mandatory_Palestine "1947–1948 civil war in Mandatory Palestine"), followed by the internationalised [1948 Arab–Israeli War](/wiki/1948_Arab%E2%80%93Israeli_War "1948 Arab–Israeli War"). A bomb by the Jewish militia group [Irgun](/wiki/Irgun "Irgun") went off in the Ramla market on 18 February, killing 7 residents and injuring 45.[[62]](#cite_note-63)[[63]](#cite_note-64)[[64]](#cite_note-65)

After a number of unsuccessful raids on Ramla, the Israeli army launched [Operation Dani](/wiki/Operation_Dani "Operation Dani"). Ramla was captured on 12 July 1948, shortly after the capture of Lydda. The Arab resistance surrendered on July 12,[[65]](#cite_note-66) and most of the remaining inhabitants were [expelled](/wiki/1948_Palestinian_expulsion_from_Lydda_and_Ramle "1948 Palestinian expulsion from Lydda and Ramle").[[66]](#cite_note-67) A disputed claim, advanced by scholars including [Ilan Pappé](/wiki/Ilan_Papp%C3%A9 "Ilan Pappé"), characterizes this as [ethnic cleansing](/wiki/Ethnic_cleansing "Ethnic cleansing").[[67]](#cite_note-68) By November 1949, there were about 2,000 Arabs in Ramla and Lod.[[68]](#cite_note-69)

### State of Israel

Ramla became a mixed Jewish–Arab town within the state of Israel. Arab homes of those who left in Ramla were given by the [Israeli government](/wiki/Politics_of_Israel "Politics of Israel") to Jews, first Holocaust refugees from Europe and then immigrants from Arab and Muslim countries.[[69]](#cite_note-Golan2003-70) In February 1949, the Jewish population was over 6,000. Ramla remained economically depressed over the next two decades, although the population steadily mounted, reaching 34,000 by 1972.[[69]](#cite_note-Golan2003-70)

A 2013 Israeli police report documented that the Central District ranks fourth among Israel's seven districts in terms of drug-related arrests.[[70]](#cite_note-71) Today, five of [Israel's prisons](/wiki/Israel_Prison_Service "Israel Prison Service") are located in Ramla, including the maximum-security [Ayalon Prison](/wiki/Ayalon_Prison "Ayalon Prison") and the country's only women's prison, called [Neve Tirza](/wiki/Neve_Tirtza_Women%27s_Prison "Neve Tirtza Women's Prison").[[71]](#cite_note-72) In 2015, Ramla had one of Israel's highest crime rates.[[72]](#cite_note-73)

## Landmarks

### White Tower

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a2/White_Tower%2C_Ramla_%28Dez._2006%29.jpg/250px-White_Tower%2C_Ramla_%28Dez._2006%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:White_Tower,_Ramla_(Dez._2006).jpg)

The [White Mosque](/wiki/White_Mosque_(Ramla) "White Mosque (Ramla)"), built in the 13th century

Main article: [White Mosque, Ramla](/wiki/White_Mosque,_Ramla "White Mosque, Ramla")

The [Tower of Ramla](/wiki/Tower_of_Ramla "Tower of Ramla"), also known as the White Tower, was built in the 13th century. It served as the minaret of the White Mosque (*al-Masjid al-Abyad*) erected by Caliph Suleiman in the 8th century, of which only remnants are to be seen today.[[73]](#cite_note-74) The tower is six stories high, with a spiral staircase of 119 steps.[[74]](#cite_note-75)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c3/Arcs_pool_IMG_5391.JPG/250px-Arcs_pool_IMG_5391.JPG)](/wiki/File:Arcs_pool_IMG_5391.JPG)

Ramla's Pool of Arches

### Pool of Arches

The Pool of Arches, also known as St. Helen's Pool and Bīr al-Anezīya, is an underground water cistern built during the reign of the Abbasid caliph [Haroun al-Rashid](/wiki/Haroun_al-Rashid "Haroun al-Rashid") in 789 [CE](/wiki/Common_Era "Common Era") (in the Early Muslim period) to provide Ramla with a steady supply of water.[[75]](#cite_note-76) Use of the cistern was apparently discontinued at the beginning of the tenth century (the beginning of the [Fatimid](/wiki/Fatimid "Fatimid") period), possibly due to the fact that the main [aqueduct](/wiki/Aqueduct_(water_supply) "Aqueduct (water supply)") to the city went out of use at that time.[[76]](#cite_note-77)

### Great Mosque

The Crusaders built a cathedral in the first half on the 12th century, converted into a mosque when the [Mamluks](/wiki/Mamluk_Sultanate_(Cairo) "Mamluk Sultanate (Cairo)") conquered Ramla in the second half of the 13th century, when they added a round minaret, an entrance from the north, and a [mihrab](/wiki/Mihrab "Mihrab"). The [Great Mosque](/wiki/Great_Mosque_of_Ramla "Great Mosque of Ramla") of Ramla, also known as the El-Omari Mosque, it is in architectural terms Israel's largest and best-preserved Crusader church.[[77]](#cite_note-78)

### Franciscan church and hospice

The Hospice of St [Nicodemus](/wiki/Nicodemus "Nicodemus") and [St Joseph of Arimathea](/wiki/St_Joseph_of_Arimathea "St Joseph of Arimathea") on Ramla's main boulevard, Herzl Street, is easily recognized by its clock-faced, square tower. It belongs to the [Franciscan](/wiki/Franciscan "Franciscan") church. [Napoleon](/wiki/Napoleon_I_of_France "Napoleon I of France") used the hospice as his headquarters during his Palestine campaign in 1799.

Further information: [St. Nicodemus and St. Joseph of Arimathea Church](/wiki/St._Nicodemus_and_St._Joseph_of_Arimathea_Church "St. Nicodemus and St. Joseph of Arimathea Church")

### Ramla Museum

The Ramla Museum is housed in the former municipal headquarters of the British Mandatory authorities. The building, from 1922, incorporates elements of Arab architecture such as arched windows and patterned tiled floors. After 1948, it was the central district office of the [Israeli Ministry of Finance](/wiki/Ministry_of_Finance_(Israel) "Ministry of Finance (Israel)"). In 2001, the building became a museum documenting the history of Ramla.

### British Commonwealth War Cemetery

The [Commonwealth War Cemetery](/wiki/Ramleh_Commonwealth_War_Graves_Commission_cemetery "Ramleh Commonwealth War Graves Commission cemetery") is the largest of its kind in Israel, holding graves of soldiers fallen during both World Wars and the British Mandate period.[[78]](#cite_note-79)

## Archaeology

### Identification

A tradition reported by [Ishtori Haparchi](/wiki/Ishtori_Haparchi "Ishtori Haparchi") (1280–1355) and other early Jewish writers is that Ramla was the biblical [Gath](/wiki/Gath_(city) "Gath (city)") of the [Philistines](/wiki/Philistines "Philistines").[[79]](#cite_note-80)[[80]](#cite_note-Mazar-81) Initial archaeological claims seemed to indicate that Ramla was not built on the site of an ancient city,[[81]](#cite_note-82) although in recent years the ruins of an older city were uncovered to the south of Ramla.[[82]](#cite_note-83) Earlier, [Benjamin Mazar](/wiki/Benjamin_Mazar "Benjamin Mazar") had proposed that ancient Gath lay at the site of Ras Abu Hamid east of Ramla.[[83]](#cite_note-84) [Avi-Yonah](/wiki/Michael_Avi-Yonah "Michael Avi-Yonah"), however, considered that to be a different Gath, usually now called Gath-Gittaim.[[84]](#cite_note-AviYonah-85) This view is also supported by other scholars, those holding that there was, both, a Gath (believed to be *[Tell es-Safi](/wiki/Tell_es-Safi "Tell es-Safi")*) and [Gath-rimmon](/wiki/List_of_minor_biblical_places#Gath-rimmon "List of minor biblical places") or [Gittaim](/wiki/List_of_minor_biblical_places#Gittaim "List of minor biblical places") (in or near Ramla).[[85]](#cite_note-86)[[86]](#cite_note-87)

### Excavation history

Archaeological excavations in Ramla conducted in 1992–1995 unearthed the remains of a dyeing industry (*Dar al-Sabbaghin*, House of the Dyers) near the White Mosque; hydraulic installations such as pools, subterranean reservoirs and cisterns; and abundant ceramic finds that include glass, coins and jar handles stamped with Arabic inscriptions.[[87]](#cite_note-88) Excavations in Ramla continued into 2010, led by Eli Haddad, Orit Segal, Vered Eshed, and Ron Toueg, on behalf of the [Israel Antiquities Authority](/wiki/Israel_Antiquities_Authority "Israel Antiquities Authority") (IAA).[[88]](#cite_note-89)

In January 2021, archaeologists from the [Hebrew University of Jerusalem](/wiki/Hebrew_University_of_Jerusalem "Hebrew University of Jerusalem") and [Haifa University](/wiki/University_of_Haifa "University of Haifa") announced the discovery of six engravings on 120,000-year-old [aurochs](/wiki/Aurochs "Aurochs") bone near the city of Ramla in the open-air Middle Paleolithic site of Nesher Ramla. According to  archaeologist Yossi Zaidner, this finding was definitely the oldest in the [Levant](/wiki/Levant "Levant"). Three-dimensional imaging and microscopic analysis were used to examine the bone. The six lines ranged in length from 38 to 42 millimeters.[[89]](#cite_note-90)[[90]](#cite_note-91)[[91]](#cite_note-92)

## Geology

### Earthquakes

The city has historically suffered severe damage from several major earthquakes, including the [1033 Jordan Rift Valley earthquake](/wiki/1033_Jordan_Rift_Valley_earthquake "1033 Jordan Rift Valley earthquake"), [1068 Near East earthquake](/wiki/1068_Near_East_earthquake "1068 Near East earthquake"), smaller earthquakes in 1070 and 1546, and the [1927 Jericho earthquake](/wiki/1927_Jericho_earthquake "1927 Jericho earthquake").[[92]](#cite_note-93)

### Rare cave ecosystem

Main article: [Ayalon Cave](/wiki/Ayalon_Cave "Ayalon Cave")

In May 2006, a naturally sealed-off underground space now known as **Ayyalon Cave** was discovered near Ramla, outside [Moshav](/wiki/Moshav "Moshav") [Yad Rambam](/wiki/Yad_Rambam "Yad Rambam"). The cave sustains an unusual type of ecosystem, based on bacteria that create all the energy they need chemically, from the sulfur compounds they find in the water, with no light or organic food coming in from the surface. A bulldozer working in the Nesher cement quarry on the outskirts of Ramla accidentally broke into the cavern. The finds have been attributed to the cave's isolation, which led to the evolution of a whole [food chain](/wiki/Food_chain "Food chain") of specially developed organisms, including several previously unknown species of [invertebrates](/wiki/Invertebrates "Invertebrates"). With several large halls on different levels, it measures 2,700 metres (8,900 ft) long, making it the third-largest [limestone](/wiki/Limestone "Limestone") [cave](/wiki/Cave "Cave") in Israel.[[93]](#cite_note-94)

One of the finds was an eyeless scorpion, given the name *[Akrav israchanani](/wiki/Akrav_israchanani "Akrav israchanani")* honouring the researchers who identified it, Israel Naaman and Hanan Dimentman. All ten specimen of the blind scorpion found in the cave had been dead for several years, possibly because recent overpumping of the groundwater has led the underground lake to shrink, and with it the food supply to dwindle. Seven more species of [troglobite](/wiki/Troglobite "Troglobite") [crustaceans](/wiki/Crustacean "Crustacean") and [springtails](/wiki/Springtail "Springtail") were discovered in "Noah's Ark Cave", as the cave has been dubbed by journalists, several of them unknown to science.[[94]](#cite_note-95)

According to the [Israel Central Bureau of Statistics](/wiki/Israel_Central_Bureau_of_Statistics "Israel Central Bureau of Statistics") (CBS), a total of 63,462 people were living in Ramla at the end of 2004. In 2001, the ethnic makeup of the city was 80% Jewish, 20% Arab (16% Muslim Arabs and 4% [Christian Arabs](/wiki/Christian_Arabs "Christian Arabs")).[[95]](#cite_note-96) Ramla is the center of [Karaite Judaism](/wiki/Karaite_Judaism "Karaite Judaism") in Israel.[[96]](#cite_note-97)

Most Jews from [Karachi](/wiki/Karachi "Karachi"), [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan"), have migrated to Israel and have resettled in Ramle, where they have built a synagogue named Magen Shalome, after the [Magain Shalome Synagogue](/wiki/Magain_Shalome_Synagogue "Magain Shalome Synagogue") from Karachi.[[97]](#cite_note-98)

## Economy

According to CBS data, there were 21,000 salaried workers and 1,700 self-employed persons in Ramla in 2000. The mean monthly wage for a salaried worker was [NIS](/wiki/New_Israeli_Shekel "New Israeli Shekel") 4,300, with a real increase of 4.4% over the course of 2000. Salaried males had a mean monthly wage of NIS 5,200, with a real increase of 3.3%, compared to NIS 3,300 for women, with a real increase of 6.3%. The average income for self-employed persons was NIS 4,900. A total of 1,100 persons received unemployment benefits, and 5,600 received income supplements.

Nesher Israel Cement Enterprises, Israel's sole producer of cement, maintains its flagship factory in Ramla.[[98]](#cite_note-99) Archaeological excavations on the grounds of the Nesher quarry have discovered the remains of a large Second Temple Jewish town and the Christian Byzantine settlement established over it. Over fifty underground hiding complexes were found in the area, some used for storage and others believed to be hiding places for humans with access to water.[[99]](#cite_note-100)

## Infrastructure

### Transportation

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fb/Ramleh_Station_circa_1930.jpeg/250px-Ramleh_Station_circa_1930.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Ramleh_Station_circa_1930.jpeg)

Original Ramla station building, c. 1930

[Ramla Railway Station](/wiki/Ramla_Railway_Station "Ramla Railway Station") provides an hourly service on the [Israel Railways](/wiki/Israel_Railways "Israel Railways") [Tel Aviv–Jerusalem line](/wiki/Jaffa%E2%80%93Jerusalem_railway "Jaffa–Jerusalem railway"). The station is located in north east side of the city and originally opened in April 1891, making it the oldest active railway station in Israel.[[100]](#cite_note-101) It was most recently reopened on April 12, 2003 after having been rebuilt in a new location closer to the town's center.

### Education

According to CBS, there are 31 schools and 12,000 students in the city. These include 22 elementary schools with a student population of 7,700 and nine high schools with a population of 3,800. In 2001, 47% of Ramla's 12th grade students graduated with a *bagrut* matriculation certificate. Many of the Jewish schools are run by Jewish orthodox organisations.

The Arabs, both Muslims and Christian, increasingly depend on their own private schools and not Israeli governmental schools. There are currently two Christian schools, such as Terra Santa School, the Greek Orthodox School, and there is one Islamic school in preparations.

The *Owpen House* in Ramla is a preschool and daycare center for Arab and Jewish children. In the afternoons, Open House runs extracurricular coexistence programs for Jewish, Christian, and Muslim children.[[101]](#cite_note-102)
